days.title: What is Cybersecurity + the CIA Triad
days.subtitle: Before any tool, any language, any certificate — every security decision traces back to three words. Today you learn them as a working lens, not a flashcard definition.
days.takeaway: Understand that security is the protection of Confidentiality (privacy), Integrity (accuracy), and Availability (uptime).
days.incidentDetail: Equifax leaked 147M records (Confidentiality breach). Stuxnet modified Iranian centrifuge controllers (Integrity breach). Dyn DNS DDoS took Twitter and Netflix offline (Availability breach).

days.title: Types of Hackers & Attack Categories
days.subtitle: You can't defend what you don't understand. Today: how attackers are categorized by intent and method, using real cases — not movie tropes.
days.takeaway: Classified threat actors by motive and mapped attack workflows using the Cyber Kill Chain model.
days.incidentDetail: Albert Gonzalez stole 170M+ card numbers (Heartland breach). Target attackers used HVAC vendor credentials to breach POS systems over weeks.

days.title: Linux Fundamentals I: Filesystem & Permissions
days.subtitle: Every pentest tool, every SOC server, every CTF box runs on Linux. Today you stop "knowing some commands" and start understanding the system the way attackers and defenders both have to.
days.takeaway: Mastered Linux directory hierarchy, permissions mask (chmod/chown), and the security implications of SUID-root binaries.
days.incidentDetail: Qualys discovered a memory corruption bug in the pkexec SUID-root binary, allowing local privilege escalation on nearly all Linux distributions for 12 years.

days.title: Linux Fundamentals II: Users, Processes & Shell
days.subtitle: Files and permissions were "what's stored." Today is "who's logged in and what's running" — the two questions every SOC analyst asks first when something looks wrong.
days.takeaway: Analyzed background processes, process tree structures, user sessions, and process mimicking evasion techniques.
days.incidentDetail: TeamTNT cryptojackers renamed malicious miner processes to 'kworker' to camouflage them as standard Linux kernel threads, evading visual detection.

days.title: Bash Scripting Basics
days.subtitle: A one-liner you type once is a habit. A script you write once runs a thousand times. Today you write your first real scripts — and learn why a single Bash bug once broke half the internet.
days.takeaway: Wrote automation scripts using loops, conditions, and parsed inputs. Learned the danger of environment variable parsing.
days.incidentDetail: A vulnerability in Bash parsed environment variables poorly, letting remote attackers run arbitrary code over CGI scripts, affecting 500M+ devices.

days.title: Networking I: The OSI Model
days.subtitle: Every packet, every exploit, every Wireshark capture you'll ever look at maps onto 7 layers. Today you stop saying "the network" as one blob and start seeing the stack.
days.takeaway: Mapped network interactions onto the 7-layer OSI model to diagnose where vulnerability vectors and defenses reside.
days.incidentDetail: WannaCry infected systems via EternalBlue, using SMB (Layer 7 Application) over port 445 (Layer 4 Transport) to route across networks (Layer 3).

days.title: Networking II: TCP/IP & Ports
days.subtitle: Yesterday you learned the 7-layer map. Today we slow right down and actually understand the two things that map is built on top of — TCP/IP, and ports — explained in plain language, step by step, so it actually sticks.
days.takeaway: Grasped TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), state changes, port structures, and port scanner reconnaissance vectors.
days.incidentDetail: Canadian teenager MafiaBoy launched SYN floods to exhaust port connections on major websites like Yahoo, eBay, and CNN, causing $1.2B in damages.

days.title: Networking III: DNS, HTTP/HTTPS & DHCP
days.subtitle: These three protocols run silently behind literally every internet action you take — every website you load, every IP address your device gets assigned. Today you understand what's actually happening, step by step, so you can spot when it's being abused.
days.takeaway: Learned domain hierarchy (DNS resolution), secure data transmission (SSL handshake), and automatic IP configuration (DHCP DORA process).
days.incidentDetail: DNS records for baidu.com were hijacked at the registrar level, redirecting traffic to a page controlled by the Iranian Cyber Army for several hours.

days.title: Subnetting & Routing Basics
days.subtitle: Networks aren't one giant flat blob — they're divided into controlled segments. Today you understand how that division works, why the Target breach happened because of it, and how to use it in labs and interviews.
days.takeaway: Calculated subnet masks (CIDR) and designed segregation protocols to segment POS networks from untrusted contractor segments.
days.incidentDetail: Lack of subnet segmentation between an HVAC contractor's portal and target POS servers allowed attackers to pivot laterally, stealing 40M cards.

days.title: Python for Security I
days.subtitle: Python is the #1 language in cybersecurity — used by attackers, defenders, and bug bounty hunters alike. Today you learn the syntax that matters and write your first actual security-oriented scripts.
days.takeaway: Wrote basic network and parser scripts in Python. Evaluated risks of downloading package dependencies.
days.incidentDetail: Attackers uploaded typosquatted packages like 'request' (instead of requests) to PyPI. Installing them executed scripts to steal tokens/wallets.

days.title: Python for Security II: Sockets & Automation
days.subtitle: Build network tools from scratch using Python sockets — the interface behind port scanners, reverse shells, and automated traffic engines.
days.takeaway: Constructed banner-grabbing and multi-threaded port scanning tools in Python; analyzed offensive automation signatures using the Mirai botnet architecture.
days.incidentDetail: Automated socket-based scanning over Telnet port 23 allowed Mirai to compromise millions of default-configured IoT devices, bringing down DNS providers globally.

days.title: Windows Internals: Registry, Processes & Event Logs
days.subtitle: Discover where attackers hide, how they persist, and how defenders detect them using core Windows Registry paths and default event logs.
days.takeaway: Audited Registry Run keys for persistence, identified core Windows process trees, and parsed Event IDs for logon anomalies and log clearing events.
days.incidentDetail: Malicious code injected into trusted SolarWinds DLLs evaded detection by mimicking normal update processes and abusing weak event log monitoring configurations.

days.title: Virtualization & Your Home Security Lab
days.subtitle: Every lab you've done on TryHackMe runs in someone else's environment. Today you build your own — a personal isolated network where you can break, attack, defend, and experiment freely with zero risk to anything real.
days.takeaway: Designed and deployed an isolated home security lab using VirtualBox with Kali Linux (attacker) and Metasploitable 2 (target) on a Host-Only virtual network; used for authorized vulnerability assessment and penetration testing practice.
days.incidentDetail: A hardware vulnerability in Intel processors that allows a malicious virtual machine running in a guest environment to read memory belonging to the hypervisor or other guest virtual machines.

days.title: Kali Linux Tools: Nmap, Wireshark, Metasploit, Burp Suite
days.subtitle: Day 13 built the lab. Today you actually use it — four tools that appear in every professional penetration test, every SOC investigation, and almost every technical interview for a security role.
days.takeaway: Executed Nmap scans (service/OS/vuln), captured traffic in Wireshark, exploited vsftpd 2.3.4 with Metasploit, and intercepted HTTP requests with Burp Suite on Metasploitable.
days.incidentName: Bangladesh Bank SWIFT Heist / Lazarus Group (2016)
days.incidentDetail: Lazarus Group installed Wireshark inside Bangladesh Bank and captured unencrypted SWIFT traffic for weeks, learning the exact protocol format and authentication flow. They then crafted fraudulent transfer requests that stole $81 million.

days.title: Phase 1 Complete: Review, Quiz & Phase 2 Preview
days.subtitle: Ten days of foundations — from CIA triad to your first exploit. Today you consolidate everything, test yourself, and look ahead at what Phase 2 brings.
days.takeaway: Completed a comprehensive review of Days 1-14, tested knowledge with quiz questions, and previewed Phase 2 (reconnaissance, exploitation, and active directory).
days.incidentDetail: Reviewed CIA triad, hacker types, Linux fundamentals, bash scripting, networking (OSI/TCP/IP/DNS/HTTP/subnetting), Python security, Windows internals, home lab setup, and Kali toolchain.

days.title: Cryptography I: Hashing vs Encryption
days.subtitle: Cryptography is the backbone of everything secure on the internet. Before you can break it, you must understand the three core operations: hashing, symmetric encryption, and asymmetric encryption.
days.takeaway: Understood the difference between hashing (one-way, integrity) and encryption (two-way, confidentiality); identified common hash algorithms and their vulnerabilities.
days.incidentDetail: LinkedIn stored passwords as unsalted SHA-1 hashes. When 167M password hashes were leaked, most were cracked within days using rainbow tables and GPU clusters because SHA-1 was fast and there was no salt.

days.title: Cryptography II: Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption
days.subtitle: Symmetric = one key to lock and unlock. Asymmetric = a public key to lock, a private key to unlock. Today you understand when each is used and why the internet needs both.
days.takeaway: Compared AES (symmetric, fast, bulk data) vs RSA (asymmetric, slow, key exchange); learned how TLS handshakes use both to establish secure connections.
days.incidentDetail: A buffer over-read in OpenSSL's Heartbeat extension leaked up to 64KB of server memory per request, including private RSA keys. Attackers could decrypt previously captured TLS traffic. 500K+ servers affected.

days.title: Cryptography III: SSL/TLS Certificates & PKI
days.subtitle: The padlock icon in your browser is backed by one of the most sophisticated trust infrastructures ever created. Today you understand exactly what that certificate means, how a Certificate Authority works, and how the entire system can be subverted.
days.takeaway: Analyzed X.509 certificate chains, understood PKI trust hierarchy, verified certificate revocation, and simulated a self-signed CA in the home lab.
days.incidentDetail: A Dutch Certificate Authority was compromised by attackers who issued 531 fraudulent certificates for domains including google.com, torproject.org, and CIA.gov. Iranian users were MITM'd for months using a fake Google certificate signed by the compromised CA.

days.title: Authentication vs Authorization
days.subtitle: Who are you? and What are you allowed to do? — these are two separate questions that get confused constantly. Today you learn why confusing them is one of the most common root causes of critical CVEs.
days.takeaway: Differentiated authentication (verifying identity) from authorization (granting permissions); analyzed real-world authN/authZ bypass cases including IDOR and broken session management.
days.incidentDetail: A former AWS employee exploited a misconfigured WAF that allowed SSRF to the AWS metadata service. The real vulnerability was authorization — the IAM role attached to the WAF had access to S3 buckets containing 100M+ credit applications. Authentication was fine, but authorization was broken.

days.title: Web Recon: Directory Busting & Technology Fingerprinting
days.subtitle: You know a website's front door exists. Today you find every side-door, maintenance hatch, backup stairwell, and forgotten window — the hidden paths attackers use to bypass logins and inject malware.
days.takeaway: Used gobuster and ffuf for directory enumeration; leveraged WhatWeb and Wappalyzer for passive technology fingerprinting; discovered hidden admin panels and tech stack CVEs.
days.incidentDetail: Magecart Group found an unsecured staging admin path on ba.com via directory busting. They injected a 23-line JavaScript skimmer into the payment page, capturing 380,000 payment card details over 2 weeks. BA was fined £20M under GDPR.

days.title: OWASP Top 10 & Web App Security Fundamentals
days.subtitle: The single most-referenced list in appsec interviews — and the foundation every bug bounty hunter, pentester, and secure developer builds on. Today you stop reciting it like a poem and start using it as a mental model.
days.takeaway: Named all 10 OWASP Top 10 categories grouped by three mental-model buckets (Bad Input Handling, Bad Access Decisions, Bad Hygiene). Exploited IDOR and SQL injection on OWASP Juice Shop and documented findings in pentest-report format.
days.incidentDetail: Equifax was running Apache Struts with CVE-2017-5638, a publicly disclosed RCE vulnerability. Attackers exploited the unpatched framework to exfiltrate 147M records. Root cause: OWASP #06 — Vulnerable and Outdated Components.

days.title: OWASP Top 10 Overview
days.subtitle: The OWASP Top 10 is the baseline checklist for web security. Today you understand all 10 categories so every vulnerability you see later has a category you already know.
days.takeaway: Understood all 10 OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories and their real-world risk rankings; mapped to attack and defense strategies.
days.incidentName: OWASP Top 10 — Shift in Risk Landscape
days.incidentDetail: The OWASP Top 10 for 2021 introduced 'Insecure Design' as a new category, moving beyond implementation bugs to emphasize that security must be architected from the start of the SDLC.

days.title: SQL Injection Deep Dive
days.subtitle: SQL injection is the oldest, most destructive web vulnerability. Today you write actual injection payloads, understand why parameterized queries stop them, and test against a real database.
days.takeaway: Executed SQL injection attacks (UNION-based, error-based, blind) against a test database; understood how parameterized queries and prepared statements prevent injection.
days.incidentDetail: Albert Gonzalez used SQL injection to breach Heartland's payment processing systems, inserting malformed SQL queries through web forms to extract 130M credit card numbers.

days.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
days.subtitle: XSS turns a trusted website into a delivery vehicle for malicious scripts. Today you write and test stored, reflected, and DOM-based XSS payloads.
days.takeaway: Exploited stored, reflected, and DOM-based XSS in a controlled lab; implemented Content-Security-Policy headers to mitigate script injection.
days.incidentDetail: Magecart attackers injected JavaScript through an XSS vulnerability on BA's payment page to steal payment card details from 380K transactions.

days.title: CSRF & Session Attacks
days.subtitle: Modern web apps use cookies and tokens to remember who you are. Today you learn how attackers forge requests and steal sessions — and how to prevent both.
days.takeaway: Demonstrated Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) and session hijacking attacks; implemented anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookie attributes.
days.incidentDetail: A CSRF vulnerability in Facebook allowed attackers to post on victims' timelines without consent by crafting malicious image tags that executed authenticated requests.

days.title: Broken Authentication & Access Control
days.subtitle: Authentication proves who you are. Access control proves what you can do. When either breaks, attackers become administrators. Today you test both.
days.takeaway: Tested authentication bypass techniques including credential stuffing, MFA fatigue, and JWT manipulation; analyzed access control failures in RBAC implementations.
days.incidentDetail: Many JWT libraries default to 'none' algorithm acceptance, allowing attackers to forge tokens by changing the algorithm header to 'none' and sending an empty signature.

days.title: Security Misconfiguration
days.subtitle: Default credentials, verbose error messages, open S3 buckets, directory listing enabled — the simplest mistakes cause the biggest breaches. Today you scan for them.
days.takeaway: Performed security misconfiguration audits across web servers, databases, and cloud storage; identified default credentials, open ports, and exposed configuration files.
days.incidentDetail: Verizon left an internal S3 bucket publicly accessible containing 14M customer records including names, PINs, and call logs due to misconfigured bucket policies.

days.title: File Upload & SSRF Vulnerabilities
days.subtitle: File upload forms and server-side requests are two of the most abused features. Today you learn how attackers weaponize both.
days.takeaway: Tested file upload filters (extension, MIME, magic byte bypasses) and executed Server-Side Request Forgery against internal metadata endpoints.
days.incidentDetail: A misconfigured AWS WAF allowed SSRF to the EC2 metadata service, retrieving IAM role credentials that granted read access to S3 buckets containing 100M+ records.

days.title: Firewalls, IDS, IPS Explained
days.subtitle: Firewalls decide who gets in. IDS watches what happens once they're in. IPS stops it automatically. Today you configure all three.
days.takeaway: Configured iptables firewall rules, analyzed Snort IDS alerts, and tested IPS rule sets that block malicious traffic patterns in real-time.
days.incidentDetail: Researchers found a critical firewall bypass vulnerability in PAN-OS that allowed unauthenticated remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on exposed management interfaces.

days.title: VPNs & Proxies
days.subtitle: Encrypted tunnels and anonymous relays are the backbone of safe remote access and attacker anonymity. Today you build both.
days.takeaway: Set up an OpenVPN server and client configuration; configured SOCKS5 proxy through SSH tunneling; analyzed traffic routing differences between forward and reverse proxies.
days.incidentDetail: A compromised datacenter in Finland exposed NordVPN's private keys, though no customer traffic was intercepted due to perfect forward secrecy being correctly implemented.

days.title: Malware Types: Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware
days.subtitle: Malware is not one thing. Each type spreads, hides, and executes differently. Today you catalog every major malware family and how they operate.
days.takeaway: Classified malware by infection vector, propagation method, and payload behavior; analyzed real samples (within sandbox) of worms, trojans, and ransomware families.
days.incidentDetail: A Russian military cyberweapon disguised as ransomware destroyed systems at Maersk, Merck, and FedEx causing $10B+ in damages through EternalBlue propagation.

days.title: Social Engineering & Phishing
days.subtitle: The weakest link in any security chain is the human. Today you understand the psychology, build a phishing simulation, and learn to spot red flags.
days.takeaway: Built a simulated phishing campaign using GoPhish; analyzed email headers for spoofing indicators; understood pretexting, baiting, and tailgating techniques.
days.incidentDetail: Lithuanian hacker Evaldas Rimasauskas sent fake phishing invoices impersonating Quanta Computer, tricking Google and Facebook employees into wiring $100M to fraudulent accounts.

days.title: Wireless Security Basics
days.subtitle: Wi-Fi signals don't stop at walls. Today you understand WPA2/WPA3 authentication, capture handshakes, and learn how open networks leak everything.
days.takeaway: Captured WPA2 4-way handshake packets using airodump-ng; analyzed wireless encryption standards and common misconfigurations in enterprise WPA2-Enterprise deployments.
days.incidentDetail: Researchers discovered a vulnerability in the WPA2 protocol that allowed attackers within Wi-Fi range to decrypt traffic, inject malware, and hijack connections without knowing the network password.

days.title: Cloud Security Basics: AWS/Azure Shared Model
days.subtitle: The cloud is someone else's computer — and you're responsible for what runs on it. Today you learn the shared responsibility model and how to avoid the top 10 cloud misconfigurations.
days.takeaway: Mapped AWS/Azure shared responsibility model; configured IAM policies with least privilege; identified common cloud misconfigurations including public S3 buckets and overly permissive roles.
days.incidentDetail: Paige Thompson exploited a misconfigured AWS WAF to execute SSRF against the EC2 metadata service, exfiltrating 100M+ credit applications from S3 buckets using the attached IAM role.

days.title: Phase 2 Complete: Review & Mini Quiz
days.subtitle: Twenty days of core security concepts — from cryptography through cloud. Today you consolidate Phase 2 and prepare for offensive security in Phase 3.
days.takeaway: Completed comprehensive review of cryptography, OWASP Top 10, web security, malware, social engineering, wireless, and cloud security fundamentals.
days.incidentDetail: Reviewed hashing vs encryption, SSL/TLS, PKI, authN/authZ, OWASP Top 10 categories (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, broken auth, misconfiguration, SSRF), firewalls, VPNs, malware types, phishing, wireless, and cloud security.

days.title: Pentesting Methodology: Recon → Exploit → Report
days.subtitle: Professional penetration testing follows a structured process. Today you learn the full lifecycle — why skipping recon wastes time, and why skipping reporting loses clients.
days.takeaway: Mapped the standard penetration testing framework (reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation, post-exploitation, reporting) and created a pentest checklist template.
days.incidentName: Pen测试 Methodology — HackerOne Uber Bug (2017)
days.incidentDetail: A security researcher followed a structured recon→exploit→report methodology on Uber's bug bounty program, identifying an SSRF on an internal API that led to AWS IAM keys for the entire production environment.

days.title: Passive Reconnaissance & OSINT
days.subtitle: The best attacks start before you touch a single system. Today you gather intelligence from public sources — DNS records, job postings, Shodan, and Google dorking.
days.takeaway: Performed passive recon using whois, DNS enumeration (dig/nslookup), Shodan API queries, Google dorking, and social media intelligence gathering on a target domain.
days.incidentDetail: Security researchers used Shodan to discover thousands of industrial control systems (ICS) exposed to the internet without authentication, including water treatment plants and power grid controllers.

days.title: Active Recon — Nmap Basics
days.subtitle: Passive recon tells you what's possible. Active recon tells you what's actually there. Today you perform your first real Nmap scans against a target.
days.takeaway: Executed Nmap scans (TCP SYN, Connect, UDP) to discover live hosts, open ports, and running services; interpreted port states and service version banners.
days.incidentDetail: The Mirai botnet used simple Nmap-style TCP SYN scans on Telnet port 23 to identify vulnerable IoT devices across the internet, compromising millions within hours.

days.title: Nmap Advanced — Scripts & Evasion
days.subtitle: Nmap's NSE (Scripting Engine) turns a port scanner into a vulnerability assessment tool. Today you automate detection and evade firewalls.
days.takeaway: Ran NSE vulnerability and discovery scripts (vuln, exploit, auth, discovery); used decoy scans, fragment scans, and custom timing to evade IDS/IPS detection.
days.incidentDetail: Nmap's smb-vuln-ms17-010 NSE script allowed defenders worldwide to rapidly scan their networks for EternalBlue vulnerability after the WannaCry outbreak.

days.title: Vulnerability Scanning with Nessus/OpenVAS
days.subtitle: Manual scanning finds what you look for. Automated scanners find what you miss. Today you configure and run professional vulnerability scanners.
days.takeaway: Configured OpenVAS (Greenbone) vulnerability scanner; ran authenticated and unauthenticated scans against a target; analyzed severity ratings and remediation recommendations.
days.incidentDetail: Post-breach forensic scanners using Nessus identified 50+ unpatched vulnerabilities on Target's POS systems including the initial HVAC vendor compromise vector.

days.title: Intro to Metasploit Framework
days.subtitle: Metasploit is the most widely used exploitation framework in the world. Today you learn its architecture, use it to exploit a real vulnerability, and understand post-exploitation modules.
days.takeaway: Launched msfconsole; searched for and configured exploit modules; exploited vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor and unvalidated SMB service on Metasploitable 2; executed post-exploitation commands.
days.incidentDetail: Attackers used publicly available Metasploit modules for Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638, demonstrating how open-source exploitation tools enable both attackers and defenders.

days.title: Exploiting a Vulnerable VM (Metasploitable)
days.subtitle: Theory without practice is just trivia. Today you chain multiple exploits against a deliberately vulnerable Linux VM — recon, exploit, privesc, and persistence.
days.takeaway: Chained Nmap recon → Metasploit exploitation → Linux privilege escalation → persistence on Metasploitable 2, following a complete penetration testing workflow end-to-end.
days.incidentDetail: Metasploitable 2 remains the most downloaded intentionally vulnerable VM, used by universities and training programs worldwide to teach ethical hacking fundamentals.

days.title: Burp Suite Basics — Intercepting Traffic
days.subtitle: Burp Suite is the Swiss Army knife of web application testing. Today you configure the proxy, intercept and modify HTTP traffic, and understand every tool in the suite.
days.takeaway: Installed and configured Burp Suite Community Edition with CA certificate; intercepted, modified, and replayed HTTP/S requests; used Repeater and Intruder tabs for manual testing.
days.incidentName: Burp Suite in Bug Bounty Workflows
days.incidentDetail: Nearly 80% of professional bug bounty hunters cite Burp Suite as their primary tool for web testing, using proxy interception to find parameter tampering and IDOR vulnerabilities.

days.title: Practicing SQLi & XSS on DVWA
days.subtitle: DVWA is the most popular intentionally vulnerable web application. Today you apply everything from Phase 2 — SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and file upload — against real targets.
days.takeaway: Exploited SQLi, stored/reflected XSS, CSRF, and file upload vulnerabilities in DVWA at multiple security levels; wrote corresponding patches for each vulnerability.
days.incidentDetail: DVWA is referenced in over 500 academic papers on web security education, serving as the standard platform for controlled vulnerability testing in university curricula.

days.title: Privilege Escalation — Linux
days.subtitle: Getting a low-privilege shell is step one. Becoming root is step two. Today you master Linux privesc techniques: SUID, cron jobs, kernel exploits, and misconfigurations.
days.takeaway: Performed Linux privilege escalation using SUID binary abuse, writable cron scripts, kernel exploit enumeration (linux-exploit-suggester), and sudo misconfiguration analysis.
days.incidentDetail: A vulnerability in Linux's pipe mechanism allowed unprivileged users to overwrite read-only files including SUID binaries, granting root-level code execution on affected kernels.

days.title: Privilege Escalation — Windows
days.subtitle: Windows privilege escalation is a different game than Linux. Today you exploit service permissions, unquoted paths, Token Impersonation, and AlwaysInstallElevated.
days.takeaway: Enumerated Windows privesc vectors using WinPEAS and PowerUp; exploited vulnerable service paths, AlwaysInstallElevated MSI, and SeImpersonatePrivilege token abuse.
days.incidentDetail: A remote code execution vulnerability in Windows Print Spooler service allowed attackers to execute arbitrary code with SYSTEM privileges, widely exploited in ransomware campaigns.

days.title: Password Cracking — Hashcat & John the Ripper
days.subtitle: Passwords are the most common authentication mechanism and the most common failure point. Today you crack hashes using GPUs, rules, and wordlists.
days.takeaway: Used Hashcat (mode 0/1000/3200) for MD5/NTLM/BCrypt cracking with rockyou.txt; performed rule-based mutation attacks and analyzed password complexity metrics.
days.incidentName: Hashcat's Role in LinkedIn Breach Analysis
days.incidentDetail: Security researchers used Hashcat with GPU clusters to crack 90%+ of the 167M unsalted SHA-1 LinkedIn password hashes within days, demonstrating why salting is critical.

days.title: Wireless Attacks — Aircrack-ng
days.subtitle: Wi-Fi security can be broken without touching the target device. Today you capture WPA2 handshakes, crack PSK passwords, and understand enterprise Wi-Fi risks.
days.takeaway: Configured monitor mode on wireless adapter; captured WPA2 4-way handshake with airodump-ng; cracked PSK using aircrack-ng and a wordlist; tested WPS Pixie Dust attack.
days.incidentDetail: Researchers discovered a vulnerability in the WPA2 protocol that allowed attackers within Wi-Fi range to decrypt traffic, inject malware, and hijack connections without knowing the network password.

days.title: Post-Exploitation & Pivoting Basics
days.subtitle: You're inside the first system. The real target is behind another firewall. Today you use Meterpreter, SSH tunneling, and SOCKS proxying to move laterally.
days.takeaway: Used Meterpreter's autoroute and socks_proxy modules for lateral movement; established SSH dynamic port forwarding; exfiltrated data through encrypted C2 channels.
days.incidentName: Lateral Movement in the Colonial Pipeline Attack
days.incidentDetail: DarkSide attackers used compromised VPN credentials to access the IT network, then pivoted to OT systems managing pipeline operations — all without triggering network segmentation alerts.

days.title: Writing Your First Pentest Report
days.subtitle: An undelivered exploit is just a proof of concept. A good pentest report is what gets findings fixed. Today you write a professional-grade report.
days.takeaway: Authored a complete penetration test report including executive summary, methodology, findings with CVSS scoring, proof-of-concept screenshots, and actionable remediation steps.
days.incidentDetail: The Penetration Testing Execution Standard (PTES) and OWASP Testing Guide provide globally recognized frameworks for structuring professional pentest deliverables.

days.title: Intro to TryHackMe / HackTheBox
days.subtitle: Capture The Flag platforms are where theory meets hands-on practice. Today you create accounts, understand room/box categories, and solve your first challenges.
days.takeaway: Completed introductory rooms on TryHackMe (Linux Fundamentals, Nmap, Burp Suite) and HackTheBox starting point machines; documented methodology in daily logs.
days.incidentDetail: Over 1M users have joined TryHackMe, with its gamified learning model shown to improve practical security skill retention by 40% compared to traditional courses.

days.title: Solve Your First Easy HTB/THM Box
days.subtitle: Walkthroughs teach you what someone else did. Solving a box without a guide teaches you to think like an attacker. Today you do it on your own.
days.takeaway: Independently enumerated, exploited, and escalated privileges on an easy HTB machine (Blue/legacy); documented step-by-step methodology without referring to walkthroughs.
days.incidentName: HTB Box: Blue — EternalBlue in Practice
days.incidentDetail: HackTheBox's 'Blue' machine provides a safe environment to practice the MS17-010 EternalBlue exploit chain that was used in WannaCry and NotPetya global ransomware outbreaks.

days.title: CTF Strategy — Web Category
days.subtitle: Web challenges in CTFs reward methodical enumeration over brute force. Today you learn to read source code, manipulate cookies, and bypass filters systematically.
days.takeaway: Solved 5+ CTF web challenges covering PHP type juggling, JWT manipulation, SSTI, and GraphQL introspection; created a reusable web CTF methodology checklist.
days.incidentDetail: DEF CON's annual CTF finals feature web challenges that reflect real-world vulnerabilities, with past challenges mirroring Facebook Graph API flaws and Google App Engine misconfigurations.

days.title: CTF Strategy — Cryptography & Forensics
days.subtitle: CTFs force you to apply cryptographic and forensic concepts under time pressure. Today you solve crypto challenges and analyze forensic evidence dumps.
days.takeaway: Decrypted classical and modern cipher CTF challenges (Caesar, Vigenère, XOR, AES); analyzed packet captures and disk images for hidden flags and embedded evidence.
days.incidentName: Crypto CTF Techniques in Real Forensics
days.incidentDetail: The same XOR analysis and frequency analysis techniques used in CTF crypto challenges are applied by forensics analysts when decrypting ransomware-encrypted files.

days.title: Phase 3 Complete: Review & Mini Quiz
days.subtitle: Twenty days of offensive security — pentesting, scanning, exploitation, and CTFs. Today you consolidate everything and gear up for defensive operations.
days.takeaway: Completed comprehensive review of pentesting methodology, Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, DVWA, privilege escalation, password cracking, wireless attacks, and CTF strategies.
days.incidentDetail: Reviewed pentesting lifecycle, OSINT/recon techniques, Nmap (basic/advanced/scripts), vulnerability scanning, Metasploit exploitation, Burp Suite interception, DVWA attacks, Linux/Windows privesc, password cracking, wireless, pivoting, pentest reporting, THM/HTB methodology, and CTF strategies.

days.title: SOC Structure — Tier 1/2/3 Analyst Roles
days.subtitle: Security Operations Centers run on a tiered model. Today you understand what each tier does, what certifications you need, and how to progress from alert triage to threat hunting.
days.takeaway: Mapped SOC Tier 1 (alert triage), Tier 2 (incident investigation), and Tier 3 (threat hunting) responsibilities; understood escalation paths and key performance metrics.
days.incidentDetail: Microsoft's SOC processes over 24 trillion signals daily across its enterprise and cloud platforms, using the Tier 1/2/3 model to manage alert volume and escalate complex threats.

days.title: Log Analysis Fundamentals
days.subtitle: Every system generates logs. Every attack leaves traces. Today you learn to parse syslog, Apache access logs, Windows Event Logs, and firewall logs for indicators of compromise.
days.takeaway: Parsed and analyzed syslog, Apache access/error logs, auth.log, and Windows Event Logs; identified brute-force patterns, directory traversal attempts, and unauthorized access events.
days.incidentName: Log Analysis in the Uber Breach (2022)
days.incidentDetail: Post-breach log analysis revealed that an attacker accessed Uber's internal systems through a contractor's compromised HackerOne account, with evidence found in VPN authentication logs.

days.title: Intro to SIEM — Splunk / ELK Basics
days.subtitle: SIEM platforms aggregate logs from everywhere and let you search them like Google for security events. Today you set up and query your first SIEM.
days.takeaway: Deployed the ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) locally; ingested sample syslog data; wrote KQL queries to filter for failed authentications and port scan events.
days.incidentDetail: The SolarWinds SUNBURST investigation relied heavily on Splunk queries (like 'search index=* ImagePath=*solws*') to identify compromised Orion servers across thousands of organizations.

days.title: Writing Detection Queries
days.subtitle: A good SIEM is only as good as its queries. Today you write Sigma rules, Splunk SPL, and KQL queries to detect real attack patterns.
days.takeaway: Authored Sigma detection rules for common attack patterns (mimikatz usage, suspicious scheduled tasks, pass-the-hash); converted Sigma to Splunk/Elasticsearch query formats.
days.incidentName: Sigma Rules at NATO Cyber Range
days.incidentDetail: NATO's Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre uses Sigma as the standard detection rule format, enabling cross-platform threat detection across heterogeneous SIEM deployments.

days.title: Windows Event Logs Deep Dive
days.subtitle: Event IDs 4624, 4688, 4648, 1102 — these numbers are a SOC analyst's second language. Today you understand every critical Windows Event Log and what attackers try to wipe.
days.takeaway: Analyzed Windows Event Logs for logon events (4624/4625), process creation (4688), service installation (4697), and log clearing (1102); detected pass-the-hash and scheduled task creation.
days.incidentName: Event Log Tampering in the NotPetya Attack
days.incidentDetail: NotPetya's 'mischa' component executed 'wevtutil cl' commands to clear Windows Event Logs, destroying forensic evidence of its execution across 80% of infected systems.

days.title: Network Traffic Analysis — Wireshark
days.subtitle: Wireshark is the microscope of network security. Today you capture live traffic, filter for suspicious patterns, and reconstruct entire TCP streams.
days.takeaway: Captured and analyzed network traffic with Wireshark; filtered for HTTP POST/GET, DNS queries, TCP handshakes, and TLS handshake details; extracted transferred files from packet captures.
days.incidentName: Wireshark in the DPRK Lazarus Bank Heists
days.incidentDetail: Forensic analysts used Wireshark to dissect SWIFT message traffic captured inside Bangladesh Bank during the Lazarus Group heist, reconstructing the exact fraudulent transfer requests.

days.title: Intrusion Detection — Snort / Suricata
days.subtitle: Snort and Suricata are the open-source standards for network intrusion detection. Today you write custom rules and detect real malware signatures.
days.takeaway: Installed and configured Snort/Suricata on the home lab; wrote custom detection rules for malware C2 beaconing, port scans, and SQLi patterns; tested rules against captured PCAPs.
days.incidentName: Snort in the Top 10 IDS Deployments
days.incidentDetail: Snort remains the #1 deployed open-source IDS globally, with over 5M downloads and detection rules covering 40,000+ known vulnerability signatures and malware patterns.

days.title: Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) Basics
days.subtitle: Traditional antivirus checks known signatures. EDR watches behavior. Today you understand how tools like CrowdStrike and Defender detect fileless attacks.
days.takeaway: Simulated process injection, persistence mechanism creation, and LSASS dumping on a test VM; observed EDR telemetry and alert generation for suspicious behavior patterns.
days.incidentDetail: During the Hafnium Exchange attacks, CrowdStrike's EDR detected anomalous w3wp.exe process behavior within minutes, enabling rapid containment of webshell persistence across global networks.

days.title: Threat Intelligence Basics
days.subtitle: You can't defend against what you don't know is coming. Today you learn how threat intelligence feeds work, how TTPs are categorized, and how to operationalize IoCs.
days.takeaway: Configured MISP threat intelligence platform; consumed STIX/TAXII feeds; categorized IoCs (IPs, domains, hashes) and mapped them to MITRE ATT&CK techniques.
days.incidentDetail: The Malware Information Sharing Platform (MISP) enabled real-time ransomware IoC sharing across 1,000+ organizations during the 2021 Kaseya supply-chain attack, accelerating global detection.

days.title: MITRE ATT&CK Framework
days.subtitle: ATT&CK is the encyclopedia of adversarial behavior. Today you learn to navigate the matrix, map real attacks to techniques, and use ATT&CK for defensive gap analysis.
days.takeaway: Navigated the MITRE ATT&CK matrix; mapped SolarWinds attack chain to 15+ techniques across initial access through exfiltration; performed organizational ATT&CK coverage gap analysis.
days.incidentDetail: Mandiant used MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping (T1078, T1133, T1553, T1564) to attribute the 2020 SolarWinds compromise to APT29, identifying unique tooling overlaps.

days.title: Incident Response Lifecycle
days.subtitle: When a breach happens, there's no time to learn. Today you drill the NIST IR lifecycle — Preparation, Detection, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned.
days.takeaway: Applied the NIST SP 800-61 incident response lifecycle to a simulated ransomware scenario; executed containment procedures (network isolation, account suspension) and documented findings.
days.incidentName: NIST IR Framework in Colonial Pipeline Response
days.incidentDetail: Colonial Pipeline's IR team followed the NIST lifecycle — isolating OT systems (containment), rebuilding from clean backups (eradication/recovery), and implementing MFA post-incident.

days.title: Digital Forensics Basics
days.subtitle: Forensics is the scientific method applied to incident investigation. Today you create forensic images, recover deleted files, and build a timeline of attacker activity.
days.takeaway: Created bit-for-bit forensic images using dd and FTK Imager; recovered deleted files with foremost and scalpel; built activity timeline using file system metadata and log correlation.
days.incidentDetail: Digital forensics revealed attackers had access to Sony's systems for over a month, with evidence of data exfiltration through encrypted channels and subsequent hard drive wipes using customized tools.

days.title: Malware Analysis — Static Basics
days.subtitle: Before you run a suspicious file, you analyze it statically. Today you inspect PE headers, strings, hashes, and imports to determine malicious intent without execution.
days.takeaway: Performed static analysis on malware samples using pestudio, Detect It Easy, and PE-bear; analyzed PE sections, import tables, embedded strings, and compiled timestamps for suspicious indicators.
days.incidentDetail: Researchers first identified Stuxnet as a sophisticated cyberweapon through static analysis — discovering its stolen digital certificates, multiple zero-day exploits, and Siemens SCADA targeting.

days.title: Malware Analysis — Dynamic/Sandbox Basics
days.subtitle: Static analysis tells you what a file looks like. Dynamic analysis shows you what it actually does. Today you run malware in an isolated sandbox and observe behavior.
days.takeaway: Executed malware samples in Cuckoo/ANY.RUN sandbox environments; monitored process creation, file system changes, registry modifications, and network connections during execution.
days.incidentName: Cuckoo Sandbox in Banking Fraud Investigations
days.incidentDetail: European banking security teams used Cuckoo sandbox to analyze Dridex banking trojan samples, identifying C2 infrastructure patterns used across 300+ financial institution compromises.

days.title: Threat Hunting Fundamentals
days.subtitle: Threat hunting is proactive detection — finding attackers before alerts fire. Today you apply the Pyramid of Pain and hypothesis-driven hunting methodology.
days.takeaway: Conducted hypothesis-driven threat hunting (assume compromise, identify TTPs, create IoAs); searched for Living-Off-the-Land (LotL) abuse patterns in Windows and Linux logs.
days.incidentName: Hunting for Living Off the Land Binaries
days.incidentDetail: The LOLBAS project catalogs Windows binaries (powershell.exe, wmic.exe, certutil.exe) commonly abused by attackers to execute code without dropping malicious files.

days.title: Security Policies & Compliance (ISO 27001, GDPR)
days.subtitle: Security is as much about policy as it is about technology. Today you understand ISO 27001 ISMS, GDPR data protection principles, and how compliance drives security spending.
days.takeaway: Mapped ISO 27001 Annex A controls to organizational security domains; understood GDPR data subject rights and breach notification requirements; created a compliance checklist.
days.incidentDetail: Meta was fined €1.2B by the Irish Data Protection Commission for violating GDPR data transfer requirements, the largest GDPR fine in history, highlighting the financial impact of non-compliance.

days.title: Risk Assessment Basics
days.subtitle: You can't protect everything equally. Risk assessment helps you decide what to protect first. Today you perform a qualitative risk assessment using NIST frameworks.
days.takeaway: Performed risk assessment using NIST SP 800-30 methodology; identified assets, threats, vulnerabilities; calculated inherent vs residual risk scores; created risk treatment plan.
days.incidentDetail: JPMorgan Chase spends $600M+ annually on cybersecurity, with risk assessment frameworks driving budget allocation toward protecting highest-risk assets including payment systems and customer data.

days.title: Zero Trust Architecture Concept
days.subtitle: Trust no one, verify everything. Zero Trust is the dominant security architecture paradigm. Today you understand its principles and how to implement them.
days.takeaway: Understood Zero Trust pillars (identity, device, network, application, data); mapped traditional perimeter defense vs Zero Trust architecture; implemented micro-segmentation in a test environment.
days.incidentDetail: Google's BeyondCorp Zero Trust architecture eliminated VPNs entirely, basing access decisions on device posture and user identity rather than network location, handling 100K+ daily authentications.

days.title: DevSecOps & Secure SDLC Intro
days.subtitle: Security can't be bolted on at the end. DevSecOps integrates security testing into every stage of software development. Today you shift left.
days.takeaway: Integrated SAST (Semgrep) and SCA (OWASP Dependency-Check) into a CI/CD pipeline; understood DAST vs SAST differences; implemented pre-commit hooks for secret scanning.
days.incidentDetail: Etsy's DevSecOps program automated 23 security checks into their deployment pipeline, reducing vulnerability discovery-to-patch time from weeks to under 24 hours.

days.title: Phase 4 Complete: Review & Mini Quiz
days.subtitle: Twenty days of defensive security — SOC, SIEM, detection, forensics, and threat hunting. Today you consolidate Phase 4 and plan your specialization path.
days.takeaway: Completed comprehensive review of SOC operations, log analysis, SIEM querying, Wireshark traffic analysis, Snort IDS, EDR telemetry, MITRE ATT&CK, incident response, forensics, and malware analysis.
days.incidentDetail: Reviewed SOC tier roles, log analysis techniques, SIEM/Splunk queries, Windows Event Log deep dive, Wireshark traffic analysis, Snort/Suricata rules, EDR concepts, threat intelligence (MISP/STIX), MITRE ATT&CK mapping, incident response lifecycle, digital forensics, static/dynamic malware analysis, threat hunting, compliance, risk assessment, Zero Trust, and DevSecOps.

days.title: Choosing Your Path: Red/Blue/Cloud/AppSec
days.subtitle: Generalists get entry-level roles. Specialists get senior roles. Today you evaluate each cybersecurity career path and pick your specialization.
days.takeaway: Evaluated offensive (pentesting/red team), defensive (SOC/blue team), cloud security, and AppSec career paths; identified personal skill gaps and created a 90-day specialization roadmap.
days.incidentDetail: Cloud security architects ($180K avg), AppSec engineers ($165K), penetration testers ($150K), SOC analysts ($95K) — specialization significantly impacts earning potential in cybersecurity.

days.title: CompTIA Security+ — Exam Overview
days.subtitle: Security+ is the most widely recognized entry-level certification. Today you understand the exam domains, question formats, and create a study plan.
days.takeaway: Understood Security+ domains (attacks/threats, architecture/design, implementation, operations/incident response, governance/risk/compliance); created a 14-day study plan with PBQ practice.
days.incidentName: Security+ in the SOC Hiring Pipeline
days.incidentDetail: Over 60% of SOC analyst job postings list CompTIA Security+ as a preferred or required qualification, making it the most common entry credential for blue team roles.

days.title: Security+ — Domains 1 & 2 Deep Review
days.subtitle: Domain 1 covers threats and attacks. Domain 2 covers architecture and design. Today you deep-dive into indicators of compromise, threat actors, and secure network design.
days.takeaway: Reviewed Domain 1 (attacks/threats/vulnerabilities) — malware types, social engineering, application attacks, wireless attacks, and cryptographic attacks. Domain 2 — defense-in-depth, secure segmentation, and virtualization security.
days.incidentDetail: Security+ PBQ simulations test candidates on real scenarios like configuring a firewall after a phishing attack or identifying the best security control for a given risk scenario.

days.title: Security+ — Domains 3 & 4 Deep Review
days.subtitle: Domain 3 covers implementation. Domain 4 covers operations. Today you master secure protocols, identity management, incident response procedures, and forensic best practices.
days.takeaway: Reviewed Domain 3 (implementation) — PKI certificates, secure protocols, host/network security controls, identity/access management. Domain 4 (operations) — IR process, disaster recovery, data sovereignty, forensic procedures.
days.incidentDetail: A Security+ PBQ might present a compromised web server scenario requiring: disable account → collect memory dump → preserve logs → check other servers for lateral movement.

days.title: Security+ — Domain 5 + Practice Test
days.subtitle: Domain 5 covers governance, risk, and compliance. Today you master policies, risk management, and privacy concepts, then take a full-length practice test.
days.takeaway: Reviewed Domain 5 (governance/risk/compliance) — security policies, risk assessment types, business continuity, GDPR/HIPAA/FISMA compliance, vendor due diligence.
days.incidentDetail: The Security+ exam requires a score of 750/900. Domain weighting: 24% attacks/threats, 21% architecture, 25% implementation, 16% operations, 14% governance.

days.title: Intro to Bug Bounty Hunting
days.subtitle: Bug bounty programs pay for findings. Today you learn how to responsibly disclose vulnerabilities, choose programs, and write your first bug bounty report.
days.takeaway: Signed up on HackerOne/Bugcrowd; studied disclosure policies for 5 major programs; identified low-hanging vulnerabilities (missing headers, exposed .git/config, CORS misconfigurations) on target domains.
days.incidentDetail: Hackers have earned over $100M in bounties on HackerOne since 2012, with the top 10 earners making $1M+ each through consistent vulnerability research and disclosure.

days.title: Setting Up GitHub Portfolio for Security Work
days.subtitle: Your GitHub profile is your resume for technical security roles. Today you organize repos, write excellent READMEs, and demonstrate your security capabilities through code and documentation.
days.takeaway: Restructured GitHub repositories with professional READMEs, security lab documentation, and script tooling; added GitHub Actions CI/CD for automated security checks on repos.
days.incidentDetail: GitHub's Security Lab program has paid $10K+ bounties for vulnerabilities found in open-source projects, demonstrating the career value of a strong, visible security GitHub profile.

days.title: Writing a Security-Focused Resume
days.subtitle: Generic resumes don't get past security hiring managers. Today you tailor your resume with security-specific keywords, quantify achievements, and highlight hands-on lab experience.
days.takeaway: Rewrote resume with security-focused metrics (CVEs identified, vulnerabilities remediated, alerts processed); added home lab projects, CTF achievements, and TryHackMe/HackTheBox rankings.
days.incidentDetail: Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) for security roles commonly scan for: SIEM, Splunk, Wireshark, Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, Python, Bash, incident response, penetration testing, OWASP, MITRE ATT&CK.

days.title: LinkedIn & Personal Brand for Security
days.subtitle: Recruiters find you before you find them. Today you optimize your LinkedIn profile with security keywords, post build-in-public content, and network with security professionals.
days.takeaway: Optimized LinkedIn headline and about section for security roles; posted daily security learning updates; connected with 20+ security recruiters and engineers at target companies.
days.incidentDetail: LinkedIn reported a 65% increase in cybersecurity job postings in 2024, with hiring managers citing a candidate's public 'build-in-public' learning portfolio as a strong positive signal.

days.title: Mock Technical Interview — Concepts Round
days.subtitle: The first interview tests concepts. Today you practice answering fundamental security questions clearly and confidently.
days.takeaway: Practiced answering common security interview questions (CIA triad, risk management, OWASP Top 10, encryption types, incident response process, defense-in-depth) with structured STAR responses.
days.incidentDetail: Google's security engineer interviews include 4-5 rounds covering networking, OS internals, cryptography, threat modeling (STRIDE), and system design — all focused on practical application.

days.title: Mock Technical Interview — Practical Round
days.subtitle: The second interview tests tool skills. Today you perform a timed practical assessment — Nmap scan, SQLi exploitation, Wireshark analysis, and incident triage.
days.takeaway: Completed a timed practical assessment: Nmap scan target → identify vuln → Metasploit exploit → document findings. Performed Wireshark traffic analysis and event log triage.
days.incidentDetail: Mandiant's practical interview round requires candidates to analyze a real malware sample, determine C2 infrastructure from PCAPs, and write an intrusion analysis report within 2 hours.

days.title: Internship & Entry-Role Hunting Strategy
days.subtitle: Finding the first security role is the hardest step. Today you create a job search strategy targeting internships, apprenticeships, and entry-level SOC roles.
days.takeaway: Created a target company list (MSSPs, startups, SOCs) with 20+ applications planned; wrote tailored cover letters for each; set up job alerts and tracked applications in a spreadsheet.
days.incidentDetail: The NSA's cybersecurity apprenticeship program hires 100+ entry-level analysts annually with zero experience required — a 3-year paid training pipeline for government cyber roles.

days.title: Capstone Project — Plan Your Pentest Report
days.subtitle: Your capstone ties everything together. Today you plan a full penetration test against a home lab target, defining scope, rules of engagement, and testing methodology.
days.takeaway: Defined scope and rules of engagement for a full pentest against a home lab network; created test plan covering recon, scanning, exploitation, and reporting phases; got approval for controlled testing.
days.incidentDetail: 60% of security hiring managers say a capstone penetration test report is more valuable than a certification in evaluating entry-level candidates, as it demonstrates applied methodology.

days.title: Capstone Project — Execute & Polish
days.subtitle: Execution day. Today you run your full pentest, document every finding with screenshots, exploit chains, and CVSS scores, and produce a professional-grade deliverable.
days.takeaway: Executed full pentest against target: Nmap → vuln scan → get initial shell → privesc to root → maintain access → exfil simulated data → write complete report with executive summary.
days.incidentDetail: Professional pentest reports include: Executive Summary, Scope/Methodology, Risk Ratings, Detailed Findings (screenshots + CVSS), Recon Data, Exploit Chains, Remediation Steps, and Appendix.

days.title: Final Recap & Your Next 6-Month Plan
days.subtitle: Today is not the end — it's the beginning of your security career. Review everything, celebrate progress, and plan the next phase of your cybersecurity journey.
days.takeaway: Completed a comprehensive review of all 90 days of cybersecurity training across 5 phases; created a 6-month continuous improvement plan targeting certifications, labs, and job applications.
days.incidentDetail: Completing a structured 90-day cybersecurity challenge demonstrates sustained dedication, technical breadth, and documentation discipline — three traits hiring managers rank as top indicators of entry-level success.
